title: Police CAMERA Act of 2025
plain_summary: The Police CAMERA Act of 2025 creates a program where the federal government gives money to police departments to buy body cameras for their officers. Police agencies that get this money must write clear rules about how to use the cameras and how to protect people's privacy when storing video recordings. The agencies also have to report numbers about how often police use force and how many complaints people make against officers. This program is run by the Department of Justice and helps state, local, and tribal police departments set up body camera programs.
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on the Judiciary.

sections.title: Section 1  Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Police Creating Accountability by Making Effective Recording Available Act of two thousand twenty five or the Police CAMERA Act of two thousand twenty five.

sections.title: Section 2  Matching Grant Program for Law Enforcement Body-Worn Cameras
sections.text: This section changes an existing law called the Omnibus Crime Control and Safe Streets Act of nineteen sixty eight. It adds a new part that creates a grant program for police body cameras. The Director of the Bureau of Justice Assistance can give money to states, local governments, and Indian tribes. This money helps them buy or rent body cameras for police officers. The money also pays for costs related to starting a body camera program. The goal is to stop police from using too much force, make police more accountable and transparent when they use force, help respond to complaints against police officers, and improve how evidence is collected. These grants last for two years. When a grant is awarded, the Director gives fifty percent of the money right away. The other fifty percent is given after the grantee shows they have met certain requirements. The grant money goes directly to the state, local government, or Indian tribe. The money can be used to buy or rent body cameras for police officers who patrol their area. It can also pay for costs like training officers, storing recorded videos, keeping the videos safe and secure, or creating policies to follow the program rules. To get a grant, the applicant must promise to create policies and procedures before officers start using body cameras. They must also create rules for collecting and keeping recorded data before officers use the cameras. These policies and rules must be made available to the public. They must also follow rules about how recorded data can be used. The required policies and procedures must be developed with input from the community and published for everyone to see. These policies must cover how to safely and effectively use body cameras, how to securely store and handle recorded videos, how to protect the privacy of people who might be recorded, how to protect constitutional rights when facial recognition technology is used, limits on using body cameras with facial recognition technology, how to release recorded data according to state open records laws, and how to make recorded data available to prosecutors, defense attorneys, and other court officers. The entity must also regularly check how secure their storage and handling of body camera data is. The data collection and keeping rules must require officers wearing body cameras to explain if something that should be recorded is not recorded. Officers must get permission from crime victims or witnesses before recording interviews with them. The collection of recorded data not related to legitimate police work should be minimized as much as possible. The system used to store recorded data must keep a record of all viewing, changes, or deletion of stored data and must prevent unauthorized access or sharing of stored data as much as possible. Police officers cannot access stored data without an authorized reason. The police agency must collect and report statistical data on incidents where force was used broken down by race, ethnicity, gender, and age of the victim, the number of complaints filed against police officers, what happened with complaints filed against police officers, the number of times camera footage is used as evidence in crime investigations, and any other statistical data the Director decides should be collected and reported. The rules must also allow people to file complaints with police agencies about improper use of body cameras and follow any other requirements set by the Director. Statistical data must be reported to the Director, who will create a standardized reporting system and a national database of this information. Recorded data from body cameras can only be used in internal and external investigations of police misconduct, if there is reasonable suspicion that a recording contains evidence of a crime, or for limited training purposes. The Director will establish rules to make sure recorded data is only used for these purposes. Generally, entities receiving grants cannot transfer recorded data to other law enforcement or intelligence agencies. However, there are exceptions for criminal investigations if the requesting agency has reasonable suspicion that the data contains evidence related to the crime being investigated, and for civil rights investigations. The federal government will pay no more than seventy five percent of the total program cost, except for Indian tribes who can use certain federal funds for their matching portion. The Director can waive the matching requirement in cases of financial hardship. For fiscal years two thousand twenty six through two thousand twenty eight, thirty million dollars of the Bureau of Justice Assistance budget will be used for this program. Within two years of this law being enacted, the Director of the Office of Audit, Assessment, and Management will assess the grant program and the policies of grant recipients. Starting two years after this law is enacted, each grant recipient must submit an annual report by September first describing the progress of their body camera program and recommendations for further support. The Director of the Office of Audit, Assessment, and Management will review grantees' policies and take necessary steps to ensure program compliance. The Director will create and maintain a toolkit for police agencies, universities, and other relevant groups to provide training and technical assistance, including best practices, model policies and procedures, and research materials. The Director can combine research, practices, templates, and tools that have been developed by experts and police agencies across the country. To request a grant, the chief executive of a state, local government, or Indian tribe must submit an application to the Director in the required form. Within ninety days of this law being enacted, the Director will create regulations to implement this program, including what information must be included in applications and what requirements states, local governments, and Indian tribes must meet when applying. Within two years after all grants are awarded, the Director must conduct a study on the effectiveness of body cameras in preventing excessive force by police officers, the impact on accountability and transparency of police use of force, the impact on responses to and decisions about excessive force complaints, the effect on officer safety during patrol, the effect on public safety, the impact on evidence collection for criminal investigations, issues with secure storage and handling of recorded data, privacy issues for citizens and officers recorded on cameras, constitutional rights issues when facial recognition technology is used, limitations on facial recognition technology use, public access to body camera footage, the need for proper officer training, best practices for developing safe and effective use protocols, a review of police agencies that found body cameras unhelpful, and any other factors the Director considers relevant. Within one hundred eighty days after completing the study, the Director must submit a report to Congress with any policy recommendations they consider appropriate.
